pairs.note: Indefinite article cannot be used with a definite noun suffix.
Don’t combine the indefinite article with the definite suffix (-et).
A definite suffix belongs to a definite noun phrase; remove it in the indefinite.
Keep the noun indefinite if you use 'ett'. Definite would be 'äpplet' (without ett).
With adjective + definite noun, add det/den/de (and keep the noun definite).
In definite NPs, the noun typically carries the definite suffix too (double definiteness).
A singular count noun usually needs an article in an indefinite NP.
With a single, countable item, include en/ett.
Omitting the article sounds incomplete unless you mean an uncountable/general reading.
For ett-words in singular indefinite, the adjective often takes -t (stort).
For en-words in singular indefinite, the adjective usually stays in the base form (stor).
In definite noun phrases, the adjective commonly uses the -a form (stora).
